coredata.dc:title: Next-generation nanomaterials-based biosensors: Real-time biosensing devices for detecting emerging environmental pollutants

coredata.dc:description: Over the last few decades, industrialization and urbanization have accelerated environmental degradation and, therefore, require scientific approaches for proper monitoring and management. This review specifically examines biosensors based on nanomaterials and their application in environmental monitoring for sustainable purposes such as detecting heavy metals in water, air-borne pollutants and greenhouse gases, pesticides in soil and foods, identification of biological agents and pathogens. For instance, graphene based immunosensors have shown high level of resistivity and stability, identifying lead ions in water at very low limits of detection, 0.01 ppb. In the same way, biosensors, which contain gold nanoparticles, have shown high sensitivity to the mercury ions; their detection limit reaches 0.005 ppb. This review describes the nanomaterials used in biosensors in terms of their dimensionality and the essential properties of mechanical, thermal, electronic, optical, and catalytic that qualify them for use in biosensors. It also presents the mechanisms of interaction between nanomaterials and biomolecules based on the intended application, and other important factors that need to be considered when designing nanobiomaterials. Furthermore, the review highlights the current issues, limitations, and future prospects of the material in terms of stability, reproducibility, biocompatibility, and environmental concerns, as well as the integration of the material into future smart multi-functional systems such as Internet of Things (IoT), machine learning (ML), and other innovative systems, and issues related to scaling up and commercialization. This systematic literature review shows the potential role of nanomaterial biosensors to enhance the achievement of the sustainable development goals (SDGs) toward a healthier and sustainable society.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the last few decades, industrialization and urbanization have accelerated environmental degradation and, therefore, require scientific approaches for proper monitoring and management. This review specifically examines biosensors based on nanomaterials and their application in environmental monitoring for sustainable purposes such as detecting heavy metals in water, air-borne pollutants and greenhouse gases, pesticides in soil and foods, identification of biological agents and pathogens. For instance, graphene based immunosensors have shown high level of resistivity and stability, identifying lead ions in water at very low limits of detection, 0.01 ppb. In the same way, biosensors, which contain gold nanoparticles, have shown high sensitivity to the mercury ions; their detection limit reaches 0.005 ppb. This review describes the nanomaterials used in biosensors in terms of their dimensionality and the essential properties of mechanical, thermal, electronic, optical, and catalytic that qualify them for use in biosensors. It also presents the mechanisms of interaction between nanomaterials and biomolecules based on the intended application, and other important factors that need to be considered when designing nanobiomaterials. Furthermore, the review highlights the current issues, limitations, and future prospects of the material in terms of stability, reproducibility, biocompatibility, and environmental concerns, as well as the integration of the material into future smart multi-functional systems such as Internet of Things (IoT), machine learning (ML), and other innovative systems, and issues related to scaling up and commercialization. This systematic literature review shows the potential role of nanomaterial biosensors to enhance the achievement of the sustainable development goals (SDGs) toward a healthier and sustainable society.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of industrialization and urbanization worldwide has led to significant environmental degradation, necessitating innovative approaches for effective monitoring and management. In India, for instance, the Green Revolution's extensive use of agrochemicals, including pesticides and fertilizers, has resulted in soil and water contamination, affecting both agricultural productivity and human health. Moreover, many Indian rivers, such as the Ganges, suffer from pollution due to untreated industrial effluents containing heavy metals and dyes from textile industries. Similarly, in China, rapid industrialization has caused severe air pollution, with cities like Beijing frequently experiencing high levels of particulate matter (PM2.5) and greenhouse gases. The Yangtze River, crucial for China's water supply, faces significant pollution from untreated industrial discharges, leading to high concentrations of heavy metals and other pollutants. In the United States, the Midwest's heavy use of fertilizers and pesticides in farming has contaminated groundwater and surface water bodies, contributing to the Gulf of Mexico's "Dead Zone." Additionally, industrial regions such as the Rust Belt have a history of soil and water contamination due to pollutants from steel manufacturing and other heavy industries. In Brazil, deforestation in the Amazon rainforest and the use of agrochemicals in expanding agricultural frontiers lead to soil degradation and contamination of water bodies with pesticides and fertilizers. Urban areas like São Paulo also face significant air pollution challenges due to industrial emissions and vehicular exhaust, contributing to high levels of particulate matter and greenhouse gases. The excessive use of agrochemicals, including pesticides, insecticides, herbicides, and fertilizers, poses global environmental and health risks. Continuous application of chemical fertilizers leads to soil acidification and loss of fertility, while pesticides harm beneficial soil microorganisms, disrupting soil health and biodiversity. Agricultural runoff carries these chemicals into nearby water bodies, contaminating rivers, lakes, and groundwater, affecting aquatic life and posing risks to human health through the consumption of contaminated water. Industrial activities generate effluents containing hazardous substances, including heavy metals, dyes, and gaseous by-products. The inadequate treatment and disposal of these effluents exacerbate environmental pollution. Heavy metals such as lead, mercury, cadmium, and chromium from industrial effluents are toxic to both aquatic and terrestrial life, accumulating in the food chain and posing serious health risks to humans. Textile and chemical industries discharge effluents with synthetic dyes and organic pollutants, which can be toxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic [1]. [2]. In the present era, where on one side development is fostered solely through industrial growth, whereas, on the other side our planet Earth is brawling with various environmental hassles, accounting for climate change, habitat loss and ecosystem degradation worldwide [3]. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop a potent environmental monitoring system to detect, inspect and evict the hazardous emerging pollutants, inducing ecological balance and diminishing the allied consequences. Previously conducted chemical and spectrophotometric analysis through gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), while audacious in their performance, are restricted by their efficiency, cost, operational system and energy consumption [4].

The fabrication of modernized monitoring devices that offer high sensitivity, accuracy, affordability, and ease of operation is an absolute necessity in today's world. As environmental challenges grow more complex, the need for swift, sensitive, and real-time monitoring devices has become increasingly critical. These devices must have the capability to detect and analyze the bioavailability of emerging pollutants in various environments effectively. The demand for such advanced devices has driven the invention of ultra-modern biosensing technology. These cutting-edge biosensors are designed to meet the rigorous requirements of contemporary environmental monitoring. Their high sensitivity allows for the detection of minute concentrations of pollutants, which is essential for early warning systems and timely intervention. For instance, detecting trace amounts of heavy metals in water or low levels of airborne pollutants can prevent widespread contamination and mitigate potential health risks. Moreover, the accuracy of these biosensors ensures reliable data, which is crucial for informed decision-making and regulatory compliance. Accurate monitoring helps in assessing the effectiveness of pollution control measures and in formulating policies for environmental protection. Affordability is another critical aspect, as cost-effective biosensors make widespread deployment feasible, especially in resource-limited settings. Affordable devices can be used in a variety of applications, from urban air quality monitoring to agricultural soil analysis, without imposing a significant financial burden. Effortless operational systems are designed to make these devices user-friendly, reducing the need for specialized training and enabling broader adoption. Modern biosensors often feature automated processes and digital interfaces, which simplify data collection and analysis. Integration with wireless technologies and the Internet of Things (IoT) allows for real-time data transmission and remote monitoring, enhancing the responsiveness and efficiency of environmental management strategies. Ultra-modern biosensing devices incorporate advanced nanomaterials, which enhance their performance. For example, graphene-based sensors offer exceptional electrical properties and surface area, improving sensitivity and detection limits. Similarly, gold nanoparticles are used for their excellent biocompatibility and catalytic properties, which are vital for detecting specific biological agents and chemical pollutants. The development of these sophisticated biosensors addresses the urgent need for effective environmental monitoring tools. They provide a comprehensive solution for understanding, detecting, and analyzing the presence of pollutants, thereby contributing to environmental sustainability and public health protection. As technology continues to advance, these devices will play an increasingly pivotal role in safeguarding our environment [5,6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of biosensors in environmental monitoring
ce:section.ce:para:
Biosensors are sophisticated analytical devices designed to detect and measure biological substances. They operate on the principle of converting biological signals into quantifiable optical, thermal, or electrical signals [7,8]. This transformation process involves several key components: a bioreceptor, a transducer, and an electronic system that includes a signal amplifier, processor, and display. The bioreceptor is a critical element of the biosensor, responsible for the specific interaction with the analyte—the substance to be measured. Bioreceptors can be enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, cells, or other biological materials that have a high affinity for the target analyte. This interaction is highly selective, ensuring that the biosensor can detect the presence of the analyte even in complex mixtures and at very low concentrations [7,8]. Once the bioreceptor interacts with the analyte, the transducer plays its role. The transducer converts the biological interaction into a measurable signal. Depending on the type of biosensor, this signal can be optical, thermal, or electrical.
•
Optical Biosensors: These detect changes in light absorption, fluorescence, or luminescence resulting from the interaction between the bioreceptor and the analyte. For example, when a target molecule binds to a fluorescently labeled antibody, the change in fluorescence intensity can be measured.
•
Thermal Biosensors: These measure changes in temperature caused by exothermic or endothermic reactions during the interaction between the bioreceptor and the analyte. These temperature changes are then converted into an electrical signal.
•
Electrical Biosensors: These detect changes in electrical properties, such as impedance, conductance, or current, resulting from the interaction between the bioreceptor and the analyte. For instance, when a biological reaction alters the electrical conductivity of a material, this change can be detected and quantified.

The signal processing system amplifies and processes the signal generated by the transducer. It filters out any noise or irrelevant information, ensuring that the final output is accurate and reliable. The processed signal is then displayed in a user-friendly format, allowing for easy interpretation and analysis.

One of the key advantages of biosensors is their high precision. They are capable of detecting minute amounts of analytes with great accuracy. This is crucial in applications such as environmental monitoring, where detecting trace levels of pollutants can prevent ecological damage and protect public health. Biosensors can detect pollutants at parts-per-billion (ppb) or even parts-per-trillion (ppt) levels, making them indispensable tools for ensuring environmental safety. Furthermore, the versatility of biosensors allows them to be used in various fields beyond environmental monitoring, including medical diagnostics, food safety, and bioprocess control [8,9]. Their ability to provide rapid, accurate, and on-site analysis makes them valuable for real-time monitoring and decision-making.

Biosensors have been classified into various categories based on bioreceptor, transducer, integration with emerging technology and detection system as summarized in Fig. 1 [10]. The biosensing technique has achieved a notable milestone in a very short period due to its versatility in recognizing an array of analytes including emerging pollutants, food additives, microbes, drugs, etc. [9]. The aforementioned peculiarity of biosensors provides ample opportunities in diverse fields such as environmental sensing, pharmaceutics, food, defense, forensic, medicine, robotics, etc. Biosensors fabricated for environmental monitoring are affected by intricate ecological matrices, troublesome for the detection of particular pollutants. Hence, biosensor assemblage for environmental monitoring requires precise detection and fabrication material which can cope with the difficulties imposed by complicated ecological aggregation [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensor's applications for achieving sustainable developmental goals (SDGs)
ce:section.ce:para: Biosensors play a pivotal role in achieving various Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) through their application in environmental monitoring and management (Fig. 2 ). Their high sensitivity and specificity make them invaluable tools for ensuring clean water, responsible consumption, climate action, and the protection of life both below water and on land. For SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation), biosensors significantly contribute by detecting and filtering emerging pollutants in water bodies, ensuring accessibility to clean drinking water and providing a safe environment for aquatic ecosystems. These devices can identify trace levels of contaminants such as heavy metals, pesticides, and pathogens in real-time, enabling timely intervention and remediation. This capability is crucial for preventing waterborne diseases and maintaining the health of aquatic habitats. In terms of SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), biosensors facilitate the delimitation of hazardous components and litter in specimens for initial treatment and instrumental examination. They help manage consumption and production processes more efficiently, reducing harmful impacts on flora and fauna. By identifying and quantifying dangerous substances in raw materials, products, and waste streams, biosensors enable industries to optimize processes, minimize waste, and ensure that hazardous substances are treated before disposal. This approach not only protects the environment but also promotes sustainable industrial practices. Supporting SDG 13 (Climate Action), biosensors aid in energy conservation and reducing the carbon footprint associated with traditional chemical analysis methods. Conventional chemical analyses often require substantial amounts of reagents, energy, and time, whereas biosensors offer rapid and efficient detection with minimal resource consumption. Integrating biosensors with Internet of Things (IoT) platforms allows continuous monitoring of environmental parameters, leading to more efficient energy use and lower greenhouse gas emissions. This transition towards sustainable monitoring practices is crucial for mitigating climate change.

Biosensors are also critical for achieving SDG 14 (Life Below Water) by governing pollution remediation in marine ecosystems. They monitor a wide range of marine pollutants, including oil spills, heavy metals, and microplastics, providing real-time data that informs pollution control strategies. This helps protect marine biodiversity and ensures the sustainability of fisheries and other marine resources. Detecting mercury contamination in seawater at very low concentrations allows for early intervention, reducing the impact on marine life. For SDG 15 (Life on Land), biosensors restrict the accumulation and action of emerging pollutants in terrestrial ecosystems. They detect pollutants such as pesticides, heavy metals, and organic contaminants in soil and air, thereby protecting terrestrial flora and fauna. Accurate and timely data provided by biosensors enable better management of land resources, preventing soil degradation and loss of biodiversity. This is particularly important for maintaining healthy ecosystems and supporting sustainable agriculture [12,13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensor's applications for environmental monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Biosensors for environmental monitoring of various hazardous pollutants include genosensors, immunosensors, aptasensors and enzymatic sensors with developing recognition sites correspondingly with nucleic acids, antibodies, aptamers, and enzymes (Table 1 ). Table 1 shows that nucleic acids are often employed in biosensors for their high specificity in recognizing target sequences. For example, optical biosensors using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and evanescent-wave optical fibers are effective in detecting Legionella pneumophila and mercury, respectively. Fluorescence-based optical biosensors also show high sensitivity for mercury detection. Additionally, electrochemical voltammetric biosensors are a reliable option for mercury analysis, demonstrating the versatility of nucleic acids in various biosensing platforms. Antibodies, both monoclonal and polyclonal, are widely used in biosensors for their ability to bind specifically to target antigens. For instance, electrochemical voltammetric and amperometric biosensors are designed to detect atrazine, a common pesticide. SPR-based optical biosensors and electrochemical amperometric biosensors are effective in identifying Legionella pneumophila. Electrochemiluminescence and amperometric biosensors are utilized for detecting Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis, respectively. Other notable applications include the detection of microcystin, okadaic acid, domoic acid, and 17 β-estradiol through various electrochemical and optical methods, underscoring the extensive applicability of antibody-based biosensors.

Aptamers, synthetic oligonucleotides that bind to specific targets with high affinity and specificity, are employed in a range of biosensors. These include optical (colorimetric) and electrochemical (impedimetric) sensors for detecting acetamiprid and atrazine. Fluorescence-based optical biosensors for lead detection and impedimetric sensors for brevetoxin-2 showcase the adaptability of aptamers in different sensing technologies. Interferometry-based optical sensors for saxitoxin and fluorescence-based sensors for bisphenol A highlight the diverse applications of aptamer-based biosensors. A photo-electrochemical biosensor is also noted for the detection of 17 β-estradiol. Enzymes are commonly used in biosensors due to their catalytic properties and specificity for substrates. Amperometric and colorimetric biosensors are effective in detecting paraoxon, a toxic organophosphate. Electrochemical impedimetric sensors for methyl parathion and voltammetric sensors for chlorpyrifos illustrate the broad application of enzyme-based biosensors in pesticide detection. Additionally, fluorescence and impedimetric biosensors for dichlorvos, voltammetric sensors for pirimicarb, and amperometric sensors for carbofuran and carbaryl emphasize the critical role of enzymes in environmental monitoring. The inclusion of a fluorescence-based optical sensor for lead detection further demonstrates the versatility of enzyme-based biosensors.

Modernized biosensors possess a magnificent ability to detect a wide range of emerging pollutants. Heavy metals (HMs) are the most obnoxious pollutants of the present industrial era posing myriads of issues. Contamination of aquatic environments on exposure to heavy metals is a topnotch concern as these metals are non-biodegradable and can persist in soil and water bodies for innumerable years [15,16]. Heavy metals including chromium (Cr), lead (Pb), arsenic (As), mercury (Hg), copper (Cu), and zinc (Zn) are indispensable components of various industries viz. electroplating, paints and varnishes, leather tanning, automotive machinery, paper production, electronics, etc. [16,17]. Besides, anthropogenic activities including mining are also a major source of HM discharge in the environment. The ultimate fate of these metals is their transport and accumulation in water and soil, thereafter entering the food chain [16,18]. Moreover, fatal diseases transmitted by HM-contaminated drinking water intimidated various establishments such as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA) and World Health Organization (WHO) to strictly furnish guidelines for an admissible limit of HM content in the drinking water. These HMs once enter the biological system may induce oxidative stress by aggravating the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as well as down-regulates the antioxidant metabolism [19,20] Likewise, application of agrochemicals viz. pesticides and fertilizers, plant growth regulators and other repellents, for enhancing crop yields and conferring protection from various diseases classified as another category of environmental pollutants. Excessive usage of agrochemicals leads to their accumulation in the surface water as well as seepage into the drinking water, thus threatening the ecosystem balance and human health [21,22].

The conventional analytical detection techniques for HMs include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XPS) [23]. The analytical methods for agrochemical detection involve gas chromatography coupled with mass spectroscopy (GC-MS), liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) viz. are lodged with various limiting factors such as inaccuracy as well as involved unsafe practices [23,24]. This boosts the operational system of biosensors and more precisely electrochemical biosensors [25]. Advancement in biosensor designing has been segregated into five major generations: the first generation biosensor that was developed by Leland Charles Clark Jr. for the first time to compute signals generated by a) analyte mixture and b) the product obtained through bioreceptor reactions. These were further modified by various scientists including the fabrication of the first enzyme electrode and, the production of potentiometric electrode sensors, thermistors, and optical biosensors [10]. In the second-generation biosensors, supplementary enzymes and additional reactants were introduced to significantly impact the analytical output of biosensors and they were termed mediator amperometric biosensors [26,27]. The third to fifth-generation biosensors were fabricated to create the direct interface, evading any intermediates, amongst receptors and electrodes conducted through electron transfer. These generation biosensors had an edge over the earlier ones in being economical and reproducibility with high precision [28].

Along with other emerging technologies, the biosensing technique also requires advancement with progression in its application to various fields. Nanotechnology has transcended other techniques and taken hold of innumerable fields viz. physics, chemistry, biosciences, agriculture, science and industry, medical health care, aerospace, energy, electronics, environmental science, etc. The propensity of nanotechnology to orchestrate and modify various materials at the nano range and then interpret and comprehend their functionality creates new horizons for biosensor advancement [29]. Moreover, the prime and foremost problem of global climate change can only be tackled with green technology and nanomaterials are the best players in this regard. It is observed that nano-dimensioned materials are highly effective as compared to their bulk counterparts [30].

Fig. 3 shows the timeline for several publications from the year 2004–2024 available in Scopus using different keywords. Fig. 3(a) gives the graphical representation of publications with keywords nanomaterial and biosensors portraying a total of 3, 453 documents. Fig. 3(b) represents a total of 134 documents with keywords nanomaterial, biosensors and environmental monitoring. From this data, it could be analyzed that the nanomaterial in biosensor fabrication has been studied and explored extensively by the scientific community, but the scientific data is still lagging regarding the application of nanomaterial-based biosensors in environmental monitoring. Hence, to fill the gap and draw the attention of researchers toward significant and effective outcomes of NMs in environmental monitoring, an effort has been made by providing information on sustainable solutions for environmental monitoring via NM's enabled biosensors, through this review article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Merits and demirts of biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Agriculture, public security, ecological protection, and healthcare are the key sectors in which biological sensors are playing their application role. The likelihood of high sensitivity and better outcomes can be significantly increased using biosensors with optical properties [31]. This could also open the door for accelerating the disease monitoring process and other possibilities. Most significantly, biosensors are needed to advance the medical research, health care, and pharmacological industries. These biological sensors provide access to modern, cutting-edge analytical instruments. Many sensitive augmentation methods have been developed to increase the signal-to-noise proportion of biosensors. The biosensor characteristics such as resilience, repeatability, sensibility, brevity, and longevity should be taken into consideration while its uses in real-world applications [32]. The optical biosensor outperforms other detection technologies, particularly in regards to versatility and assessment velocity. The rapid detecting speed is advantageous in all applications. Biosensors with optical technology can deliver real-time dynamic data to their consumers. Optical biosensors have a wide range of uses, including civil, army, robotics, and safety for the public, decreased testing expenses, non-invasive evaluation, and the security of food quality. The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) model is used in optical biosensors for two reasons i.e. easy to fabricate and has excellent sensitivity [33]. In the biological sensor industry, machine learning (ML) is used as an instrument or strategy for data processing and interpretation, such as retrieving characteristics or anticipating analyte types and their concentration level. Novel sophisticated machine learning algorithms, particularly the technique of deep learning, which is well-known for analyzing images, recognition of faces, and recognition of speech, have stayed rather elusive in the sensor discipline. An additional choice is to employ biosensors that are worn and machine learning to track wellbeing. Wearable biological sensors have sparked widespread interest due to their enormous opportunity for non-invasive assessment of human biology via various bodily fluids (such as tears, sweat, and saliva) [34]. Disparately integrating several sensor arrays on stretchable patches to continually monitor biomarkers over time is an ideal objective for wearable biosensors. The state of health can be accurately determined by using machine learning to analyze the time sequence of concatenated data collected by sensors. ML needs to be explicable in these programs, not a mysterious black box. Medical practitioners and individuals making decisions need to be able to comprehend the machine's conclusion. In the interim, a deep learning system must transparently include human understanding and deductive principles to enforce and control its learning and decision-making process. Furthermore, the sample size required to train the algorithm for machine learning can be greatly decreased by incorporating human understanding and deductive processes into machine learning [35]. Excellent monitoring limits, resilience, ease of restructuring and manufacture, quicker detection, uniform output, and outstanding resolution are all features of biosensors with electrochemical sensors. However, they have a short or restricted lifespan, unsteady voltage and current, a restricted or limited temperatures range, and hypersensitivity when combined with other substances. Optical biosensors are compact, inexpensive, highly sensitive, and selective. But, they are also prone to influence from outside influences and physical alteration. Thermal biosensors are scalable, user-friendly, and easy to fabricate. Their drawbacks include low specificity in temperature measurements and lengthy experimentation processes. Mass-sensitive or gravimetric Wave biosensors are cost-effective and simple, but have low sensitivity. Piezoelectric biosensors are characterized by fast detection, strong response to frequency, compact dimensions, and high sensitivities. There are drawbacks, including Extreme temperature sensibility, unsuitable for static settings [36]. The nanomaterial biosensor poses significant advantages over others as they have some negative implication. The use of nanomaterial has ability to increase the power of specificity, response time, signal to noise ratio and sensitivity and make sensor more efficiently in several application such as medical field, food quality monitoring, drugs discovery, environmental monitoring etc. The use of metal nanoparticles and quantum dots increases the sensitivity and fluorescence polarization of biosensor by improving the potential of photostability and signal amplification due to low size and surface modification and specific fluorescence property [37,38]. The presence of nanoparticles in biosensor improves the biocompatibility, consequently which helps in several medical application for disease diagnosis [38]. The optical property in optical biosensor helps in increasing the detection tendency of DNA based biosensor through biosensing mechanism [39]. The nanomaterial-based biosensor poses very difficulties in synthesis as well in characterization and also shows very poor stability and reproducibility [38,40]. Several nano-biosensor exhibit toxic effect such as ecotoxic and mutagenic effect, so these are toxic in nature as well [41]. In the use of single enzyme biosensor, sensitivity of biosensor to different or multiple targets make difficulty to sense the exact concentration of several targets in the sample mixture. While in multiple enzymes, each enzyme has their specific immobilization parameters and it is a major limitation of biosensor for a multienzyme biosensor because of common method of immobilization. Multienzyme exhibit low sensitivity and stability as these have complex manufacturing and a single pH buffer use for multienzyme [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fundamentals of nanomaterials in biosensors
Overview of nanomaterials (NMs)
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials refer to particles or substances ranging on nanoscale between 1 and 100 nm, at least in one dimension out of three as well as a spherical surface area of more than 60 (m2/cm3) by volume [43]. Nanomaterials could be classified based on different criteria as listed in Fig. 4 . However, most frequently NMs are classified based on size dimensions: zero-dimensional (0D), one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D), mentioned in Fig. 4.

33.
•
0D NMs: these materials are identified by analyzing if all three dimensions (x, y, and z) are restricted to the nanoscale range i.e., less than 10 nm. They include NMs which could be in metallic or ceramic form, amorphous or crystalline (single or poly), and display numerous configurations and states. Quantum dots (QDs), fullerenes to be named some significant 0D NMs [44–46].
•
1D NMs: these NMs discerned as 1D NMs, when they exhibit two of their dimensions (x, y) on the nanoscale level and one other dimension is beyond this scale i.e., more than 10 nm. The NMs under this category include nanotubes, nanorods, nanowires, and nanofibers. The properties include crystalline (single or multi) or amorphous; ceramic, polymeric, or metallic; isolated or embedded in another medium, etc. [44–46].
•
2D NMs: they include NMs that have a single dimension (x) in the nanometer range and the other two are outside the limit of the nanometer scale. They also have multiple forms such as crystalline or amorphous; possess diverse chemical constituents; polymeric or metallic; dispersed on the substrate or embedded in the nearby matrix. They include a vast variety of NMs such as fibers, nano sheets, nano walls, tubes, free particles, multilayered thin films, etc. [44–46].
•
3D NMs: these are alternatively termed bulk materials that exceed the nanoscale range in all three dimensions i.e., they measure beyond 100 nm. However, the building blocks of these 3D NMs are in the nanometer range. Bundles of nanotubes and nanowires, multi-nano layers and nanoparticle dispersion forming nanocomposites are a few materials that come under this category of 3D NMs [44–46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of nanomaterials (NMs)
General properties
ce:section.ce:para:
The characteristic features of NMs such as exorbitant conduction properties, modest resistance to charge carrier, sturdy mechanical strength and flexibility as well as influential surface-to-volume ratio, satiate the conditions to fabricate highly efficient biosensing materials. The physicochemical properties of NMs are described below.
•
Mechanical properties: The mechanical properties of NMs are linked to their hardness, brittleness, toughness, plasticity, fatigue strength, rigidity, and tensile strength [47,48]. Brittleness is exhibited by the inorganic, non-metallic materials, whereas organic materials are highly flexible. NMs at the nanoscale level altogether show divergent properties, compared to their bulk counterparts, owe to surface and quantum impact [47]. The various interactions held in between nanoparticles (NPs) or surface and NPs that alter the mechanical properties at the nanoscale level are van der Waals forces, usual and lateral capillary interactions, electrical double layer and electrostatic forces as well as forces linked with structure, solvation and hydration [44]. The change in the mechanical properties of FeAl powder, in being brittle at the microparticle level and exhibiting fine plasticity, and a mixture of strength and ductility at the nanoscale level, was observed [44].
•
Thermal properties: The transmittance of electrons and photons, referring to lattice vibration as well as their scattering phenomenon are the driving forces behind heat transfer in NPs [49]. The nanosized particles exhibit excellent electrical and thermal conductance as with diminishing size, there is a hyperbolic enhancement in the NP's surface area to volume ratio. Since heat transfer is linked with electron conductance, the enhanced surface-to-volume ratio in NPs aggravates the content of electrons for heat transfer, lacking in their bulk counterparts [50,51]. In addition, the process of micro convection arising from the Brownian motion of NPs also induces thermal conduction, only when solid NPs get dispersed in a liquid medium forming nanofluids [52,53].
•
Electronic and optical properties: The phenomenon of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) as well as quantum confinement induces characteristics like photoluminescence, non-linear optical properties, and linear absorption in metallic and semiconductor NPs [54]. The LSPR phenomenon, happening when incident photon frequency corroborates with collaborative excitation of the conductive electrons, bestows robust size-related UV–visible extinction band which is not found in their bulk parts. The optical properties of NPs including reflection, transmission, absorption, and light scattering are highly versatile and vary remarkably form their bulk equivalents. Modification of NMs in terms of structure, shape, size, surface functionalization, and composition is extremely appreciable in manipulating optical properties for a wide range of applications in desired fields. Optical properties are of key importance in encompassing the electrical characteristics of NPs manifesting quantum effect with the probability of diversity in size, shape and type influencing color they emit. For instance, gold (Au) NPs with a 25 nm diameter emerge as green-colored particles, while with a 100 nm diameter, the orange color is reflected by them [55]. The internal electronic arrangement influences the optical properties of NMs, hence important in deciphering its structure [56,57].
•
Catalytic properties: The novel catalytic properties such as reactivity and selectivity are highly efficient and frequent in NPs as compared to their bulk analogs. The catalytic qualities are dependent on the shape, composition, size, interparticle distance, and oxidation level of NPs [58]. In the case of the size of NPs, the catalytic reactivity displays an inverse relation i.e., the smaller the size, the greater the catalytic efficiency. For composition, the NPs combined with alloys exhibit superior and aggravated catalytic activity due to altered electronic properties, decreased toxic effect and specificity, conferred by the alloying effect. Moreover, compact-shaped NPs manifest vigorous and efficacious catalytic activities. The hemispherical NPs are much more efficacious than spherical NPs, likewise, nanocubes are found to be 14 times and 4 times superior in catalytic activity, respectively in comparison to nanoplates and nanospheres [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structurally induced properties of nanomaterials suitable for sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The nanomaterials for sensing applications exhibit superior properties owing to their amaller dimensions, when compared to their bulk counterparts. The size and dimensions of NMs poses significant impact on the selectivity, sensitivity, performance, and various other sensing mechanisms.

High surface area and surface-to-volume ratio: An enormous increase in the active surface area of sensors have been observed on size declination from micro to nano scale, consequently the sensing output. Moreover, miniatured NMs usually have extensive surface-to-volume ratio, elevating the sensitivity of NMs-derived sensors towards the analytes [59]. Owing to high surface-to-volume ratio, nanomaterials are capable of surpassing their adsorption abilities, elevating the mobility of the NMs in the aqueous medium facilitating prompt scanning of entire volume, conferring enlarged density of low-coordinated atoms at the surface, thus elevating surface reactivity, which elaborates the physicochemical technqiues for degradarion or immobilization of pollutants, and capacity to fabricate NMs in various sizes and shapes, thus imparting versatility for tuning their properties according to the requirement [60]. Moreover, with declining particle size, as seen in NMs when compared to their bulk counterparts, the quantum effect outshines remarkably by aligning their radius with asymptotic exciton Bohr radius (the separation gap between the hole and the electron). As a result of achieving quantum confinement effects, an array of non-magnetic materials become magnetic, besides the affinity for electrons either gaining or donating alters tremendously, impacting the catalytic behaviour of the NMs [61].

Functionalized NMs: the functional NMs involving 0D, 1D, 2D nano-entities including nanowires, nanotubes, NPs, nanosheets etc. in combination with materials exhibiting nanostructured surfaces viz., semiconductors, metals, and organic materials. They are highly suitable for developing transducers in sensing and detection devices due to extensive surface-to-volume composition as well as possessing physical attributes due to nanotip and nanogap, inducing altogether different properties, as compared to NM's bulk properties. Besides acting as transducers, these functional NMs manifest magnetic attributes, thus employed as capture agents in developing nanosensors, signal amplifiers useful for developing SERS chips, and detection elements suitable for polymers for molecular imprinting. Surface modifications is crucial for obtaining NM functionalization, particularly for sensing chips and spectral sensors [62].

Porosity and roughness: an outstanding sensing capability can be achieved via superior frictional electrical execution of various sensors. The nanomaterials having high dielectric constants are applied to enhance the porosity of the electrode materials. The porous structure exhibit extensive surface area, consequently enhanced surface charges, besides diminishing the thickness of the porous material experiencing stress, which subsequently enhances the capacitance and boosts the electrical output of the sensors [63,64]. Porous inorganic NMs and their derivatives viz., porous metal nanostructures, nano-zeolites, porous carbon NMs etc. exhibit enhanced surface-to-volume ratio, facilitating bulk transport and binding ability which is highly suitable for elaborative interaction between target analyte and immobilized molecules, thus aggravating catalysis and sensing output, particularly important for environmental monitoring [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism of nanomaterials and biomolecules
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles are capable of inducing various interactions with biomolecules due to the complexity of the biological environment where they exist. The inbuilt tendency of NPs to modify their shape, state, and structure depending upon the physical conditions of the environment including pH, organic matter content, ionic strength and amount of oxygen, makes them the most versatile material to work with biomolecules in varying environmental conditions [65]. Further, the considerable role of NPs has been observed in stabilizing the protein structure of degrading enzymes and boosting the catalytic activity of enzymes via surface immobilization [66]. Nanoparticles are unique in thoroughly entering the cellular membranes due to their nanosized, this allows them to imitate the biomolecules and follow their pathways such as carrier-enabled movement across epithelial cell barriers. The NP's properties such as charge, shape, and reactivity change significantly under the influence of protein binding, thus altering their toxicological and biological attributes [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design consideration for nanomaterial-based biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Designing nanomaterial-based biosensors involves three primary constituents: a nanomaterial, a recognition/identification segment that confers selectivity, and a signal transduction pathway that ensures the binding of the analyte to the receptor of the biosensor unit (Fig. 5 ). These components, while integral to the biosensor, are not necessarily distinct systems but are essential features that can be used to categorize nanomaterial-based biosensors. The nanomaterial component is critical as it provides the necessary platform for the biosensor. Nanomaterials, such as nanoparticles, nanowires, and nanotubes, offer unique properties like high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and optical characteristics that enhance the sensitivity and performance of the biosensor. The recognition/identification segment is responsible for the selective interaction with the target analyte. This segment can include biological molecules like antibodies, nucleic acids, enzymes, or synthetic molecules such as aptamers. These recognition elements are chosen for their ability to bind specifically to the analyte of interest, ensuring high selectivity and reducing the likelihood of false positives. The signal transduction pathway converts the recognition event into a measurable signal. This pathway can involve various mechanisms, such as electrochemical, optical, or piezoelectric methods, which translate the interaction between the analyte and the recognition element into a quantifiable output. The efficiency and accuracy of this transduction are crucial for the biosensor's overall performance.

These sensing systems can be designed for the detection of single or multiple analytes, known as singleplex or multiplex detection systems, respectively. Singleplex systems focus on detecting one specific analyte, while multiplex systems can simultaneously detect multiple analytes. This capability is particularly useful in environmental monitoring where diverse contaminants need to be identified and quantified. The analytes typically targeted by these biosensors for environmental monitoring include heavy metals such as lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), chromium (Cr), mercury (Hg), and zinc (Zn). Pathogens, including bacteria and fungi, and pesticides like organophosphates, atrazine, and carbamates, are also commonly monitored using these advanced biosensors. The ability to detect these pollutants accurately and in real-time is essential for assessing environmental health and implementing timely remediation strategies [68].

The nanoprobe is designed using various nanomaterials (NMs) such as quantum dots (QDs), metal nanoparticles (NPs), and carbon-based NMs. These materials are selected for their unique properties that enhance the performance of biosensors. Quantum dots, for instance, are semiconductor nanoparticles that exhibit size-tunable optical properties, making them excellent for fluorescence-based sensing applications. Metal nanoparticles, including gold and silver NPs, offer exceptional electrical conductivity and catalytic activity, which are beneficial for electrochemical sensing. Carbon-based NMs, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, provide outstanding mechanical strength, high electrical conductivity, and large surface areas, making them versatile for various sensing mechanisms. The high surface area to volume ratio of NMs is a critical advantage. This characteristic significantly enhances the interaction between the nanomaterial and the target analyte. The increased surface area allows for a higher density of functional groups or binding sites on the sensor surface, leading to improved sensitivity and the ability to detect exceptionally minute alterations in surface chemistry. This capability translates to highly precise measurements and tremendously low detection limits, enabling the detection of analytes at very low concentrations, which is essential for accurate environmental monitoring. Additionally, the diverse surface functional groups of NMs enable them to be easily modified or functionalized with specific recognition elements such as antibodies, nucleic acids, or enzymes. This functionalization enhances the selectivity of the biosensors, allowing them to target specific pollutants or pathogens with high precision. For example, quantum dots can be conjugated with antibodies to create highly specific fluorescence-based biosensors for pathogen detection, while metal nanoparticles can be functionalized with DNA probes for the electrochemical detection of heavy metals.

The extremely low dimensions of NMs also contribute to the development of miniaturized sensors. These nanoscale dimensions allow for the fabrication of compact and portable sensing devices, which are highly advantageous for on-site and real-time environmental monitoring. The portability of these sensors ensures that they can be easily deployed in various settings, from remote field locations to urban environments, providing immediate and accurate data on environmental conditions. Furthermore, the quick signal response reaction of nanomaterial-based sensors revolutionizes the world of sensing technologies. The rapid interaction between the analyte and the sensor surface, coupled with the efficient transduction of the signal, results in swift and reliable detection. This fast response time is crucial for applications that require immediate decision-making and timely interventions, such as detecting toxic pollutants in drinking water or monitoring air quality in industrial areas [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of nanomaterials used in biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Advances in biosensor technology are crucial for the early and accurate identification of harmful or beneficial molecules to mitigate their adverse impacts on health or treat illnesses at an early stage. A biosensor is an analytical tool that mimics biological processes to detect specific molecules (e.g., harmful chemicals or biomolecules) through a tailored interaction between these targets and sensing materials (e.g., enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, etc.). Some of the major types of biosensors classified based on sensing molecules include enzyme-based, DNA-based, and immunosensors [69]. Besides the sensing molecule itself, a multitude of biosensors suited to recognize specific target molecules have also been developed using techniques like surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance, fluorescence or electroanalysis. Early biosensors faced some drawbacks, such as the generation of weak electrical signals from the biomolecular reaction, instability of the biomolecules, and overall low sensitivity. These limitations were addressed by the development of novel materials (e.g., conducting polymers, porous materials, etc.), which can immobilize the biomolecules to improve their electron transfer reaction and preserve their bioactivity [70,71]. Recently, there has been a breakthrough in the utilization of nanomaterials for biosensors, owing to their exceptional properties, including high conductance and cellular biocompatibility. One of the key strengths of nanomaterials lies in their large surface area, which along with other unique properties, renders their wide acceptance in fields, like battery electrode research and environmental clean-up [71,72].

Recent advancements in nanomaterials have amplified their importance in biochip development for numerous biological applications, such as stem cell therapy and drug delivery. This stems from the inherent benefits of nanomaterials for biological use: (a) easy penetration into cell membranes owing to their smaller size and (b) high biocompatibility for efficient delivery of drugs and cell-differentiation inducers into cells or tissues. Particularly, in the field of biosensing, nanomaterials can be hybridized with biomolecules to create biosensors with high sensitivity. This combines the strengths of each category, leading to a synergistic effect [73]. For instance, the use of metal nanoparticles (NPs) in enzyme-based electrochemical sensors, upconverting NPs and quantum dots (QDs) in optical sensors, or polymer substrate and nanomaterials in flexible biosensors, has created a buzz in the field lately. Nanobiosensors work in a similar way to conventional biosensors, but they use tiny components on a nanometer scale to covert the biological signal into a useable reading. Nanobiosensors are superior to their traditional counterparts owing to their diverse applications in various fields: (a) medical diagnosis for the detection of biochemical markers within cells, (b) monitoring of physical or chemical phenomena in poorly accessible areas, (a) identification of exceptionally low concentrations of harmful substances, and (d) environmental monitoring through measurement of nanoscopic particles in the industrial areas or environment (Fig. 6 ). In general, the types of nanomaterials employed in biosensors can be grouped into four major categories, as detailed below under separate sub-headings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoparticles (NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles (NPs) are tiny spheres, nanometers in size, and the most versatile and commonly used nanomaterial. Their small size unlocks exceptional physical and chemical properties not seen in their traditional bulkier counterparts. NPs are revolutionizing the medicine sector and are currently useful in developing biosensors for diagnosing health issues, creating medical images, targeting precise delivery of drugs, and exploring newer therapies [74]. Nanoenzymes, created using catalytically active nanomaterials like transition metal oxides, are noted for their ability to speed up biochemical reactions on biosensors. They are majorly grouped into (a) metal NPs (e.g., platinum, gold, palladium, silver, copper, iron, cobalt, etc.) and (b) metal oxide NPs (e.g., zinc oxide [ZnO], manganese dioxide [MnO2], titanium dioxide [TiO2], tin dioxide [SnO2], etc.). Both types of NPs offer a range of impressive properties, including excellent interaction with light, electrical conductivity, magnetism, unique chemical behaviors, and most importantly, catalysis. NP-based biosensors can be fine-tuned by adding coatings made from various materials like polymers, graphene, silica networks, metal oxides, fibers, and dendrimers [75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal- and metal oxide-based NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles made up of metals and/or its oxides are highly efficient adsorbents due to their high adsorption capacities and fast kinetics. Metal oxides can serve as effective materials for building biosensors due to their several key advantages: (a) high electrocatalysis, (b) cost-effectiveness, and (c) increased ability to capture organic molecules. Some of the most commonly used metal-oxide NPs in biosensors include ZnO, TiO2, MnO2, SnO2, and oxides of copper (CuO), iron (Fe2O3), nickel (NiO), cobalt (Co3O4), cadmium (CdO), cerium (CeO2), and molybdenum (MoO3). Various nanostructures developed from metal oxides are used as gas sensors for detecting environmental pollutants such as volatile organic compounds and toxic gases based on the change in electrical conductivity on electrode surface [76]. For example, an ammonia sensor based on platinum NPs and amorphous indium-gallium-zinc oxide could detect ammonia at high levels (i.e., a value of 1467 at 250 °C and 1000 ppm of ammonia/air) with a faster response time [77]. A hydrogen-detecting sensor was created using a nanocomposite, 3D palladium-crumpled reduced graphene oxide ball (Pd-CGB), which could effectively detect hydrogen from 0.0025 to 2%. At 2% hydrogen concentration, the sensor recorded a 14.8% response value, 73 s response time, and time of recovery as 126 s [78]. A ZnO-based biosensor was created by the immobilization of acetylcholinesterase enzyme on its surface for detecting pesticides like organophosphates at a range of concentrations from 0.5 nM–5 μM [79]. Cheng et al. developed a paper-based hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detecting biosensor within a range of 2.4–150 μM using mesoporous CuO hollow spheres [80]. Li et al. (2020) developed a low-field NMR biosensor from superparamagnetic Fe2O3 NPs that could accurately identify Salmonella bacteria at very low levels, as low as 2.6 × 104 CFU/mL [81]. A gas sensor developed from ZnO NPs was highly stable, effective at detecting carbon monoxide (CO), and showed an impressive response (74% for 80 ppm CO) with response and recovery times of 21 and 70 s at 250 °C [82]. Fig. 7 provides a pictorial representation of the various nanomaterial-enabled biosensors along with their examples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphene are the most popular and commonly used carbon-based nanomaterials prized for their exceptional characteristics like high conductivity and biocompatibility [83]. CNTs are a particularly exciting type of 1D nanomaterial with incredible potential in biosensing, tissue engineering, medical diagnosis, delivery of drugs, tracking and labeling of cells, and biomolecular interactions. These tubes are majorly grouped into single-/double-/or multi-walled based on the number of sheets of carbon atoms. CNTs are unique in terms of their structure, electrical/mechanical/electrocatalytic characteristics, high chemical stability and conductivity, dirt-resistant surface, and increased surface area/volume ratio [84]. CNT biosensors are making waves in healthcare, food quality analysis, industry, and environmental monitoring for the precise detection of a plethora of things, including the levels of glucose, fructose, galactose, neurotransmitters, proteins (immunoglobulins, albumin, insulin), harmful bacteria, and even cancer biomarkers. Sensors created using multi-walled CNTs can detect biomolecules in real time with high accuracy, without the need for additional labels. Four different sensor arrays created using CNDs were used for the detection of specific antibiotics (e.g., tetracyclines, amide alcohols, aminoglycosides, macrolides) by facilitating the immobilization of amino acids on functional groups of CNDs [85]. Graphene oxide (GO), made from a single layer of graphite with functional groups containing oxygen attached to its surface, can also be used to develop biosensors because of its unique ability to be electrically stable, conductive, and easily customizable. A paper-based electrochemical sensor was developed from CNTs-graphene oxide-gold NPs, which could detect mercury ions in water samples with a detection limit of 30 nM. The sensor combined three main benefits: (a) conductivity and stability of CNTs, (b) increased surface area of graphene oxide and (c) high sensitivity of gold NPs [86].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots and other semiconductor materials
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are nanocrystals made from the breakdown of larger carbon materials like carbon fibers, CNTs, or graphene oxide. These zero-dimensional nanomaterials are particularly exciting in terms of their optical property, narrow emission spectra, increased photochemical stability, and low to zero photobleaching. QDs are a popular choice among optical biosensors and can detect a broad range of substances from organics and pharmaceutical analytes to biomolecules (e.g., proteins, amino acids, nucleic acids, etc.) and neurotransmitters. Commonly used QDs for sensors are made up of ZnS, CdSe, CdSe/ZnS, CdTe, CdTe/CdS, and graphene quantum. In these systems, a QD with a recognition element is attached to a graphene sheet. When a pollutant is present, the sensor gets activated and separates graphene from QD [87]. An electrochemical sensor made up of nitrogen-doped carbon QDs and graphene oxide was effective at detecting lead and cadmium ions in water with limits of detection as 1.17 and 7.45 μg/L, respectively used silver-graphene QDs to create an electrochemical sensor that could detect trinitrotoluene and p-nitrophenol owing to its high stability, adsorption, and charge transfer properties [88,89].

Recent research has seen a great advancement in biosensing technology with the emergence of sensitive and durable biosensors created using novel nanomaterials, i.e., two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) and MXenes. These semiconductor materials have sparked a lot of interest due to their enhanced conductivity, efficient ion transport, wide activated surface area, and minimal diffusion barrier [90]. One of the TMDs, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), is considered a highly fascinating material for developing efficient and sensitive biosensors compared to carbon-based nanomaterials like graphene. This is mainly attributed to its remarkable electrical properties, semi-conductive nature, and extensive energy band gap. Pirarath et al. demonstrated the role of MoS2 nanosheets in effectively adsorbing mercury ions with 93% removal efficiency in 300 min [91]. Likewise, MXenes are hydrophilic, stable, and biocompatible, and exhibit good electrical conductance, a large surface area, and ease of incorporating functional groups on their surface. Upconverting NPs (UCNPs) are another class of nanomaterials widely exploited for their optical properties. Notably, UCNPs are typically synthesized using lanthanide or actinide-doped transition metals and hold immense potential as fluorescent probes [92]. This has fueled the development of fluorescence biosensors like lateral flow assays (LFAs) based on UCNPs. For instance, UCNPs fluorescent aptasensor could detect polychlorinated biphenyls with high selectivity and sensitivity [93].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrid nanomaterials and nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials can be hybridized with other materials to develop hybrid nanomaterials with exceptional functionalities and employed in a wide range of scientific fields. Combining these nanomaterials results in remarkable electrochemical, plasmonic, and photoluminescent properties [94]. These hybrid nanomaterials can be used to create biosensors that are highly sensitive and functional [95]. A photoelectrochemical biosensor developed using cobalt oxide and lead sulfide NPs immobilized on an electrode made up of indium tin oxide was also developed for detecting H2O2 [96]. A zinc-based metallic organic framework (MOF) was developed by Kumar et al. for detecting and remediating vanadium ions from wastewater. The MOF exhibited a detection limit of 220 nM and high adsorption capacity of 460 mg/g [97]. Table 2 below represents a comparison of the various nanomaterials utilized for developing biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of nanomaterial-based biosensors in environmental sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The world today faces a burgeoning crisis of food, water, and energy, and the year 2030 is expected to see a rise in the demand for these resources by 40%, 30%, and 50%, respectively. Current production techniques, focused on resource extraction, use, and disposal at once, are failing us. This is mainly attributed to the problems of generation of air, water, and soil pollution due to this “take-make-dispose” approach. Environmental pollution from emerging harmful contaminants is a cause of serious concern, that threatens various ecosystems and human health. These toxic materials might include pesticides from agricultural sources, heavy metals from industrial activities, and pharmaceutical xenobiotics that are known to cause various diseases like cancers, and endocrine and reproductive disorders in humans [123]. For instance, heavy metals are an imminent threat to water sources, as they cannot be degraded or destroyed easily. Moreover, they can form soluble complexes, ultimately enhancing their bioaccumulation in the food chain. Conventional environmental monitoring methods struggle to detect low concentrations of contaminants and are often limited by constraints like reduced sensitivity, selectivity, and time-consuming sample pretreatment processes. More reliable ways of detecting these contaminants are thus critical considering their large-scale impacts. In this regard, biosensors are emerging as promising tools that offer high precision and efficiency for detecting a broad spectrum of environmental pollutants owing to their key advantages (e.g., user-friendliness, cost-effectiveness, real-time measurements of pollutant levels) [124]. Recently, a multitude of nanomaterial-based biosensors are paving the way for rapid detection and monitoring of environmental pollution, as described below (Fig. 8 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of heavy metals and metalloids in water
ce:section.ce:para: The upsurge in agricultural and industrial activities, coupled with improper waste disposal (wastewater or household waste), results in the accretion of heavy metals like mercury (Hg+/Hg2+), lead (Pb2+), copper (Cu2+), cadmium (Cd2+), etc., in different ecosystems. Thus, safeguarding the environment and human health by detecting trace concentrations of heavy metals using efficient techniques, is highly desired. To guide the limitations (e.g., time-consuming, labor-intensive) offered by traditional techniques like X-ray fluorescence spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, scientists have extensively evaluated various nanomaterial-based biosensors to develop affordable platforms for fast, sensitive, and selective routing out of heavy metals [125]. Mehta et al. devised a fluorescent probe made up of peptidyl linked to benzimidazolyl-cyanovinylidene fluorophore for detecting silver ions and silver NPs in water samples, with impressive detection limits, i.e., 0.64 ppb for the former and 1.1 ppb for the latter [126]. A fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based sensor developed using graphene oxide and silver NPs could effectively detect Hg2+ based on changes in DNA conformation due to metal ions [127]. In another study, a surface plasmon resonance biosensor created using epicatechin-stabilized silver NPs was suitable for the detection of Pb2+ with a detection limit of 1.52 μM, even in the presence of interfering metal ions [128]. Sharma et al. developed CDs (prepared from papaya waste) functionalized with ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and carbodiimide for detecting chromium with a limit of detection as 0.708 ppb [129]. Similarly, CDs synthesized from Chinese grass carp scales via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal autoclave method were effective at detecting Hg2+ ions at concentrations as low as 0.014 μmol/L [130]. Another study by Maruthupandi et al. reported that the smartphone-integrated paper-based sensor using copper nanoclusters synthesized by the sonication method was suitable for detecting Hg2+ ions (detection limit = 1.7 nM) in well and river water samples [131]. A colorimetric biosensor created using gold NPs was able to detect Pb2+ in tap water, surface rain, and pond water with detection limits of 2.950, 5.130, and 10.736 nM, respectively [132]. Feng et al. designed a specific colorimetric biosensor based on hollow carbon/MoS2 nanotubes for detecting Hg2+ ions in wastewater at concentrations as low as 2.7 nM [133].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of air pollutants and greenhouse gases
ce:section.ce:para: The exorbitant presence of harmful substances in the air, which may include gases, particulate matter, biological agents, etc., is often referred to as “air pollution”. These pollutants emanate from sources such as vehicular or industrial emissions (e.g., carbon monoxide, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, volatile organic compounds [VOCs]), agriculture (e.g., ammonia, methane), and natural sources. Nearly 6.7 million deaths are reported annually due to household or ambient air pollution. High concentrations of air pollutants can result in drastic consequences on human health. For instance, respiratory and cardiac disorders due to inhalation of >1 ppm nitrogen dioxide (NO2), sudden death in cases of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) exposure at concentrations >700 ppm, irritation of eyes, skin or body due to ammonia, etc., are some of the impacts of noxious air pollutants [134]. Considering their severe health impacts, careful detection and removal of these harmful gaseous molecules is the need of the hour. In this regard, the immense potential of nanomaterials can be exploited for the development of biosensors with sensing and contaminant removal capabilities. For example, Lee et al. investigated the ability of a gas sensor developed from ZnO tetrapod sponges for the detect of NO2 [135]. The sensor achieved a detection limit of 8.56 ppb and was effective at inducing photocatalytic degradation of the gas. A laboratory system was designed by Xu et al. utilizing a TiO2-based fluidized bed reactor for the degradation of VOCs, particularly acetaldehyde [136]. Likewise, the detection of hydrogen and VOCs using TiO2/Cu2O/CuO multi-nanolayers Lupan et al. and removal of aromatic hydrocarbons from wastewater using UiO-66-NH2/TiO2/ZnO nanocomposites Lotfi et al. has also been proposed [137,138]. In a recent study by Amu-Darko et al., H2S could be detected with 67.5% efficiency (at 50 ppm H2S) using a gas sensor developed from MOFs-derived indium(III) oxide (In2O3)/ZnO porous hollow nanocages [139]. Tai et al. achieved the removal of methanol utilizing boron-doped photoreduced graphene oxide synthesized by UV irradiation technique [140]. The metal-free photocatalyst could eliminate 80% of the VOCs (at 100 mg/m3 of methanol) and mineralize the VOCs into harmless CO2 by 90%. In another study, the synthesis of a ZnS QD-based gas sensor by hot-injection method could effectively detect acetone with high selectivity (91.1%), sensitivity (92.4%), quick response (5.5 s), and fast recovery (6.7 s), and a limit of detection as ∼1.2 ppm [141]. Recently, Xue et al. developed a gas sensor for photocatalytic mineralization of o-xylene using Ti3C2 MXene/TiO2 nanocomposite [142].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of pesticides and organic contaminants in soil and food
ce:section.ce:para: There is a heavy reliance on pesticides by farmers around the world to control weeds, insects, pests, and fungi. Pesticide use is perpetually rising, and estimates suggest that they might contribute nearly one-third share in various agricultural products used worldwide. Nevertheless, the global concern is pesticide overuse, which is responsible for groundwater pollution, the buildup of pesticides in food resources, and their effects on non-target organisms (e.g., humans, and animals). Exposure to pesticides can have adverse health impacts on humans: (i) cancer risk, (ii) induction of mutations, (iii) genetic changes, and (iv) neurotoxic effects [143]. Current approaches for the detection of pesticide residues face a lot of challenges (e.g., high apparatus costs, complex sample pretreatment, advanced operators, affected by changes in environmental conditions, inappropriateness for on-site/real-time residue detection, etc.), highlighting the need for stricter and sophisticated methods to guaranteeing safe food. In this situation, the detection methods employing nanomaterial-based biosensors can offer reliable and accurate analysis (Fig. 9 ). Moreover, the combination of recognition elements (e.g., enzymes, antibodies, imprinted polymers, aptamers, etc.) with nanomaterials on optical sensors can prove as a useful tool for on-site and rapid pesticide detection [144]. For example, two different optical sensors based on upconverting NPs and silver nano dendrites could effectively detect metribuzin and dimethoate pesticides with detection limits of 6.8 × 10−8 M and 0.002 ppm, respectively [145,146]. Kumar et al. developed a fluorescent biosensor from ytterbium(III) oxide (Yb2O3) coated with 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane that could effectively detect the pesticide, imazapyr at concentrations as low as 0.2 ppm [147]. Likewise, a fluorescent biosensor was developed by Yue et al. using Cu-modified carbon dots for the detection of thiophanate methyl in fruits [148]. Deng et al. created a colorimetric biosensor for detecting carbaryl pesticide (detection limit = 0.26 mg/kg) from gold nano-bipyramids and nanoporphyrins [zinc (4-pyridinyl)-porphyrin−dodecyltrimethylammoniumbromide; ZnTPyP-DTAB] exhibiting peroxidase-like activity [149]. Hu et al. devised a microfluidic arrays sensor based on CdTe QDs/acetylcholinesterase aerogel for detecting several organophosphorus pesticides, including deltamethrin, parathion, paraoxon, and dichlorvos with detection limits of 0.38, 0.94, 1.2, and 11.7 pM, respectively [150]. Using the chemical vapor deposition technique, Écija-Arenas et al. developed a surface plasmon resonance sensor by modification of transducer gold surface with a graphene monolayer and attachment of 1-pyrenebutyric acid to graphene [151]. The sensor could efficiently detect kanamycin (an antibiotic) residues in food with a detection limit of 285 nmol/L. A colorimetric aptasensor was synthesized by gold NPs for the detection of tobramycin antibiotic residues in human serum and milk with detection limits of 1.38 and 1.42 μM, respectively [152].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensing of biological agents and pathogens in the environment
ce:section.ce:para: An accurate identification, detection, and evaluation of pathogens is pertinent for scientific understanding, ecological monitoring, and ensuring food security. The most commonly used recognition elements for pathogen detection include lectins, antibodies, and bacterial receptors due to their ease of integration into biosensors. Recently, a newer technology, “NP-centered chemical nose” has attracted a lot of attention for detecting a plethora of variable bacterial species. The ability of this technique to effectively distinguish between mono- and polymicrobial infections translates to increased efficacy and prompt antimicrobial therapy, eliminating the requirement of lengthy and wide sample testing [125]. Further, multichannel nanosensors are exceptionally sensitive and quite effective at detecting specific bacterial strains within biofilms, in a matter of minutes. Fig. 10 provides a schematic illustration of various nanomaterial-based biosensors employed for the detection of biological agents and microbial pathogens. For example [157], proposed a surface plasmon resonance sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymers and gold NPs for the detection of aflatoxin B1 with a limit of detection of 1.04 pg/mL. An optical sensor utilizing single-walled CNTs as a suitable nanomaterial was developed by Lew et al. for detecting wound-induced hydrogen peroxide signaling in plants [158]. A MWCNT-based electrochemical sensor was used for the detection of Ganoderma boninense fungus responsible for basal stem rot in palms at concentrations as low as 0.0414 mg/L [159]. A colorimetric sensor using gold NPs modified with polyethylene glycol and dopamine was developed by Zhang et al. for testing the freshness of food [160]. The sensor changed color upon exposure to amines, with a detectable range of 1–100 μg/L and a detection limit of 2.8 μg/mL. A fluorometric biosensor based on single-walled CNTs could effectively detect ACE2-targeted viral S protein (a SARS-CoV-2 spike protein) with a limit of detection as 12.6 nM [161]. Likewise, Zhao et al. devised an electrochemical sensor from magnetic covalent organic framework/pillararene heterosupramolecular nanocomposites for detecting human norovirus with a detection limit of 0.84 copies/mL [162]. Dester et al. designed a gold NP-based colorimetric biosensor for detecting food-borne pathogens, especially Escherichia coli O157 in flour [163]. The sensor exhibited high sensitivity and could detect E. coli DNA with a detection limit of 2.5 ng/μL in just 30 min. Recently, nanozymes have been developed as artificial enzyme mimics, and they combine both the properties of nanomaterials and natural enzymes. For example, a 2D-MOF nanozyme with an electrochemical detector was able to effectively detect Staphylococcus aureus bacteria with a detection limit of 6 CFU/mL [164]. A novel smartphone-based microfluidic biosensor has also been developed utilizing the concept of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between GO and fluorescent molecules for detecting a range of foodborne bacterial pathogens [165]. Table 3 lists the fabrication method and applications of different types of nanomaterial-based biosensors in environmental sensing.
Physical properties of nanomaterials that facilitate interactions between pollutants and nanomaterial-enabled sensors.

Various environmental pollutants interact with nanomaterial-based sensors through a variety of mechanisms, as illustrated in Fig. 11 and elaborated in detail below.
1.
Fluorescence: Fluorescent nanomaterials interact with analytes such as pollutants via mechanisms that include charge transfer processes (e.g., photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular charge transfer, ligand to metal charge transfer, metal to ligand charge transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer). These interactions can result in either quenching or increase in fluorescence, and changes in the color of nanomaterials owing to their aggregation. Such changes in fluorescence due to electron or energy transfer from nanomaterials towards pollutants can influence the adsorption of pollutants on the nanomaterial surface and the degree of aggregation of nanomaterials due to change in color of the solution [212]. For instance, manganese oxide can use the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism to quench the fluorescence of gold nanoclusters. Hydrogen peroxide produced from the breakdown of acetylcholine can decompose manganese oxide, resulting in color change and recovery of fluorescence. However, organophosphorus pesticides are acetylcholinesterase inhibitors that limit the production of hydrogen peroxide and breakdown of manganese oxide, leading to a change in fluorescence. These changes can be used to detect and quantify pesticides [213].
2.
Magnetism: Magnetic nanomaterials can attract magnetic pollutants (e.g., heavy metals) via magnetic forces or adsorb them on their surface through electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bonding. Magnetic nanotubes surface functionalized with β-cyclodextrin could effectively remove heavy metals like nickel through adsorption mechanism with an adsorption rate of 103 mg/g [214]. Cyclodextrin nanosponges are effective substrates for the stabilization of magnetite nanoparticles and can form inclusion complexes with chlorinated pesticides, facilitating their removal from aqueous solution [215]. In another study, a tin oxide-carbon nitride (SnO2/C3N4) nanocomposite fabricated with magnetite nanoparticles could induce photodegradation of carbofuran pollutants with an efficiency rate of 89%.
3.
Catalysis: Catalytic nanomaterials favour chemical reactions such as oxidation and reduction for pollutant removal. They can oxidize pollutants by increasing the production of reactive oxygen species (e.g., superoxide anions, hydroxyl radicals) or act as catalysts themselves for oxidizing pollutants in the gaseous or liquid phase. Oxygen-containing groups are added to nanomaterials by splitting the reaction medium using oxidizing agents and inorganic acids in a refluxing state.
