Various metal oxide-based nanomaterials utilize oxidation methods in different phases (liquid/gas/combined) by heatin [216]g or plasma treatment in the presence of oxygen to remove gaseous pollutants and metals from air. Commonly used transition metals like iron, cobalt, cerium, vanadium, etc., have large holes that facilitate redox reactions for the transfer of electrons. Doping these metals onto the substrate materials can improve the surface area and reactivity of catalyst in oxidizing or reducing heavy metals and gases [217]. Moreover, nanomaterials can either transfer electrons to reduce the pollutants like organic compounds and heavy metals or convert them to less harmful products via hydrogenation. For example, an electrochemical sensor created using porous activated carbon and palladium nanoparticles could effectively detect heavy metals like cadmium, lead, and copper by catalyzing redox reactions and reducing the target ions at the surface of electrode [218]. The porosity of activated carbon increased the performance of the system by facilitating efficient electron transfer kinetics between the electrode and solution. Palladium nanoparticles improved the detection signal owing to their electrocatalytic activity and better conductivity. Another work by Ref. [219]described the detection of malathion pesticide using a AChE/Pt@UiO66-NH2/GCE biosensor via oxidation of thiocholine molecule and inhibition of acetylcholinesterase. In the presence of malathion, serine residue located within the active site of acetylcholinesterase is phosphorylated by the phosphorus atom of malathion, leading to the formation of stable complexes and inhibition of enzyme activity.
4.
Photodegradation: Photocatalytic degradation utilizes nanomaterials having a band gap of ∼3 eV, tunable electron-hole recombination rates, good conductivity, corrosion resistance, and semiconductor-like properties for charge separation and efficient visible light absorption. Upon activation by an energy source, nanocatalysts increase the production of reactive species (e.g., superoxide anions, hydroxyl radical) that act as powerful oxidizing agents and destroy organic contaminants like persistent organic pollutants, volatile organic compounds, polyaromatic hydrocarbons, pesticides, etc. [220]. Lately, the use of dopants such as carbon, sulfur, nitrogen, and fluorine for improving the photocatalytic performance of nanocatalysts has been the subject of many studies. These dopants can modify the electronic structure of nanocatalysts and enhance the number of active sites. Other nanomaterials like nanotubes, nanofibers, and plasmonic materials (e.g., silver or gold nanoparticles) can increase the surface area or absorption of light [221]. Using hydrogen peroxide as an energy source, titanium oxide (TiO2) photocatalysts immobilized onto steel plates were 100% effective at degrading phenol within 3 h [222]. A TiO2-based photocatalyst (Yb-TiO2-rGO) created photogenerated holes and free radical traps for strongly adsorbing phenol on its surface [223]. In another study, Zielińska-jurek et al.found that a nanocomposite prepared using TiO2 and platinum nanoparticles photodegraded phenol due to oxidation of the pollutant by nanoparticles [224]. Degradation of organic dyes such as Rhodamine B was achieved by WO3/Ag2CO3 nanocomposite due to the easier charge separation properties of nanostructure as a result of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [225].
5.
Surface plasmon resonance: Gold and silver nanomaterials are usually employed for developing colorimetric sensors based on the phenomenon of surface plasmon resonance (SPR). These nanostructures exhibit unique optical properties like SPR, which involves oscillating conduction band electrons in response to light. The resonance frequency of this oscillation is influenced by changes in the concentration of analytes that induce aggregation, precipitation, or change in conformation of nanomaterials [226]. Cadmium ions can be detected using chalcone carboxylic acid-capped silver nanoparticles through the formation of a complex between cadmium and functional group (‒OH) of capping agent. This interaction induces aggregation of nanoparticles, which is observed as color change and shift in the plasmonic absorbance peak of the nanoparticle solution [227]. In a study by Mochi et al., silver nanoparticles capped with 3-MPS [(3-mercaptopropyl) trimethoxy silane] were effective at detecting cobalt and nickel ions in water with a detection limit of 500 ppb [228]. The mechanism underlying this phenomenon is based on changes in the zeta-potential of nanoparticles and formation of coordination complex between nanoparticles and metal ions, leading to nanoparticle aggregation and changes in the SPR. Another sensor developed by chitosan-capped gold nanoparticles on BSA (bovine serum albumin) could detect mercury ions in water samples. Mercury ions can interact with the functional groups in chitosan and BSA and result in increased absorbance of the SPR of nanoparticles [229]. The plasmonic properties of metal nanoparticles are influenced by various factors such as size, arrangement, conformation, electrical resistivity, and interactions with other particles. For example, arsenic could be detected in water samples utilizing a sensor developed by Ranolazine-capped copper nanoparticles. Addition of arsenic leads to reduced adsorption and a bathochromic shift in the SPR band due to the release of ranolazine from the surface of nanoparticles and their concomitant aggregation and color change [230].
6.
Adsorption: Adsorption of pollutants relies on four different mechanisms, namely ion exchange, complexation/chelation, physical adsorption on surface, and electrostatic adsorption. In physical adsorption, the adsorbate sticks to the surface through van der Waals interactions, whereas the adsorbate forms strong chemical bonds with the adsorbent surface in chemical adsorption. The effectiveness of nanoadsorbents in absorbing organics, complex compounds, and heavy metals depends on the various types of interactions that include hydrophobic, electrostatic, π-π, acid-base, and van der Waals. For example, Wang et al. demonstrated the role of γ-cyclodextrin polymers in adsorbing organic contaminants (e.g., pesticides like fipronil, fluliprole, etc.) owing to their hydrophobicity and large molecular weight [231]. Moreover, the large surface area, small size, and surface properties of nanomaterials provide many active sites that can interact with different chemical species. An electrochemical sensor created using Mxene/carbon nanohorns/β-cyclodextrin metal-organic framework (MOF) was efficient in detecting carbendazim pesticide. This nanocomposite combined the host-guest recognition of β-cyclodextrin and high porosity of MOFs, resulting in effective pesticide adsorption with high selectivity and stability [232]. Functional groups containing oxygen can enhance the adsorption of polar molecules by nanomaterials (e.g., carbon nanotubes) owing to an increase in hydrophilicity. Diclofenac removal from wastewater was achieved using a cobalt-based ferrite and graphene oxide nanocomposite, which facilitated the interactions of functional groups on the nanostructure with the drug, facilitating its adsorption [233]. The adsorption capacity of nanomaterials is strongly influenced by the pH of the solution among other factors like concentration of adsorbate or adsorbent, suspended particles, operating temperature, surface charge, concentration of adsorbate, and contact time. For example, ALOthman et al., described that pharmaceutical pollutants are adsorbed on nanoparticle surface via a liquid-film diffusion mechanism [234]. Ketoprofen drug was maximally adsorbed by copper nanoparticles at pH 4.4 on the basis of the pKa of pollutant drug.
7.
Conductivity: Conductive nanomaterials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes can be used for pollutant removal owing to their electrical properties. Nanomaterials can interact with charged pollutants via electrostatic forces or aromatic pollutants via π-π interactions and result in changes in electrical resistance or conductance. A nanocomposite fabricated from zinc oxide nanorods and graphene was used to detect cadmium and lead ions, mainly due to the high conductivity, electron transfer, and mechanical stability of graphene as well as increased surface area for electrochemical detection provided by zinc oxide [235]. Lately, the development of vertical single-walled carbon nanotubes (VSWCNTs) through adsorption of SWCNTs functionalized with thiol groups on gold electrode has been reported to enhance the current property and electron transfer in electrodes [236]. The impedance curve was reduced in this system due to the synergistic effect of nanoparticles and VSWCNTs, favouring increased in-plane sp2 domains and electrical conductivity. Increasing concentrations of pesticides decreased the current signals due to interaction of pesticides with active target groups of acetylcholinesterase, thereby reducing enzyme activity [237]. A biosensor developed from gold nanoparticles and 3D-graphene provided a large surface area for adsorbing acetylcholinesterase, and increased the electron transfer and electro-oxidation of thiocholine. Incorporation of nafion into this electrode system (Nafion/gold nanoparticles/reduced graphene oxide/glass-carbon electrode) confers additional benefits such as good film adhesion properties, electrical conductivity, and biocompatibility. Oxidation of thiocholine produces a signal, intensity of which decreases with increasing concentrations of malathion and methyl parathion pesticides [238].
8.
Membrane filtration: Integration of nanomaterials with polymers can help in improving the characteristics of membranes and removal of pollutants by increasing the interactions with functional groups, hydrophilicity, and adsorption efficiency. Photocatalytic nanomaterials can degrade noxious pollutants through the process of photodegradation. Modification of membranes utilizing nanomaterials can increase the selectivity, hydrophilicity, strength, thermal stability, and antimicrobial efficacy of membranes, while decreasing fouling. Moreover, higher flow rates are achieved under low pressure conditions that enable the recovery and reuse of nanomaterials [239].
9.
Disinfection: Due to their improved surface area, charge separation, solution chemistry, transport mechanism, presence of active sites, and reduced generation of toxic by-products, nanomaterials are highly stable disinfecting agents for inhibiting the growth of air-/water-borne microbes. Nanomaterials can attach themselves to the lipopolysaccharide layer of the microbial cell wall, inducing oxidative stress. This in turn, leads to the breakdown of peptidoglycan layer and peroxidation of lipid membrane. Oxidation of membrane proteins alters the membrane's properties, leading to the release of cations. These cations inactivate the respiratory enzymes of bacteria through a variety of processes such as facilitating binding of functional groups (e.g., thiol) to proteins, inhibiting DNA replication, inducing DNA damage, peroxidation of proteins and lipids, and interfering with cellular respiration [240].
Fig. 12 illustrates the detection mechanisms of some of the environmental pollutants upon interaction with nanomaterial-enabled sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Traditional methods of pollutant detection vs. nanomaterial-based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Detection and monitoring of environmental pollutants presents a significant challenge that requires reliable, sensitive, and cost-friendly sensing technologies. Conventional methods like liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), etc., are commonly used for the detection of analytes. However, these techniques have drawbacks such as complexity, lengthy times, low sensitivity, poor reproducibility, need of high-cost equipments and reagents, labor-intensive analysis, pretreatment of samples, slow reaction time, bulky instrumentation, requirement of skilled personnel, non-portability, etc. For example, the requirement of a large sample size, costly cathode lamp for different elements, and flammable fuels for flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS) that detects heavy metals and trace elements in environmental samples, is a limitation. The interference due to chemical ionization, background, and spectra are other problems encountered in FAAS. Other limitations like oxidation of carbon graphite in graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy (GF-AAS) and requirement of ultra-pure argon gas in inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), further limit their applicability for in-situ, field studies, or point-of-use detection [242]. Nanomaterial-based sensors are a promising choice to overcome the limitations of traditional methods for detecting environmental pollutants. These sensors are simpler, small-sized, easier to fabricate, chemically versatile, cost- and user-friendly, as well as provide faster, sensitive, and selective pollutant monitoring with lower limits of detection. Different types of electrochemical sensors employ voltammetric (e.g., anodic stripping voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, square wave voltammetry, cyclic voltammetry) and amperometric techniques for environmental analysis [243].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and future directions
Stability and reproducibility issues in nanomaterial-based biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of nanomaterial-based biosensors is an important aspect of its commercial viability. Biosensors with good reproducibility and stability are currently in great demand. Along with consistency, the results should be of high accuracy and precision. These features of the biosensor render it the most reliable. The biosensor's signal strength decreases with age, therefore this element requires further care and attention. Furthermore, aging or instability accelerates with increasing temperature, demonstrating that aging is fundamentally temperature-dependent [244]. Firstly, the stability and reproducibility of nanomaterials necessitate strict monitoring and high throughput approaches in the development of biosensors-based nanomaterials. Second, biosensor detection accuracy relies on biorecognition components such as antimicrobial agents, cDNA, and aptamers, resulting in a single identifiable object. The accuracy of detection is a critical restriction in real-world applications. Nanomaterial-based biosensors use manufacturing optimization to improve stability while also boosting the ability to detect more contaminants. Furthermore, properly immovable biomaterials can assist biosensors based on nanomaterial in achieving higher levels of selectivity and endurance [245]. Modern graphene-based immunosensor manufacturing procedures are examined, and it is discovered that generated immunosensors exhibit increased resistance and stability [246].

Nanomaterial-based biosensors are highly responsive and prone to collapse on exposure to the air. As a result, biosensors have a short shelf life, further hindering their invention and real-world applications. Additionally, the complexities of proportions in actual existing solutions of samples have a major impact on biosensor assessment. Initial treatment is necessary before evaluation to remove such unwanted compounds which are accountable for regulating the development of biosensors based on nanomaterial for real-world applications.

Despite several biosensors based on nanomaterials coming to light each year, few of them have been effectively tested in clinical studies. The primary hurdles for nanomaterial-based biosensors are repeatability and resilience. In practice, nanomaterials with complex nanostructures are frequently utilized as biosensors to enhance both precision and sensitivity. However, the shape of these nanomaterials might change from generation to generation, resulting in low repeatability. In addition, vague interaction is unavoidable in most cases. As a result, additional efforts are required to precisely comprehend the nanomanufacturing procedure, accumulation of particles and interface interactions. Quality assurance is essential in clinical studies for guaranteeing that measurement results have acceptable repeatability and precision [247].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and environmental concerns
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are expected to have a significant influence on the high performance of a biosensor. The biocompatibility of nanomaterials is a crucial consideration when designing a biosensor that can be used to test biomolecules such as bacteria, viruses, and DNA. Regarding the implanted apparatus, the sensor chip will inevitably come into touch with complicated biological surroundings resulting in the degradation of device performance swiftly. The systematic studies of stability, encapsulation, and storage solutions of 2D material are constantly being explored to provide an effective solution. Polyether compounds can be used for in-situ encasing and grafting of nanomaterials to offer biocompatibility, extend cycle time, increase selective absorption, and prevent adsorption of proteins and agglomeration in the biological milieu. A platinum-wire-based enzymatic biosensor is biocompatible when put in brain tissues and can be used to monitor the integrity of tissue transplants. Gold nanoparticles, due to their biological compatibility and durability, can be widely used in electrochemical biosensors that serve as signaling molecules or probe carriers [244]. Nanomaterial cytotoxicity has not been investigated in most nanomaterial-enhanced sensing studies published in the literature. This demonstrates a disregard for the possible threat caused by material toxicity, as some nanomaterials are known to be fatal to human cells. The skin and lungs are the most exposed to nanomaterials through inhalation and physical touch. Therefore it is critical to explore the consequences of nanocomposite-cell engagement on these two distinct kinds of cells. The cytotoxicity of nanocomposites is primarily impacted by their chemical and physical characteristics, which are defined by the synthesis process and reagent/precursor utilized. The graphene-zirconia composite employed in this study was manufactured using a green technique that eliminated reduction procedures and harmful chemical loading. The graphene-zirconia nanocomposite is expected to have good biocompatibility due to its residual-free production technique [248].

The implementation of nano biosensors for monitoring the environment is intended to scientifically supplement and reinforce existing environmental management practices as well as the legislative framework for tracking the environment. This is about the fact that the formation of international, regional, national, state, and local environmental laws was prompted by the imperative to protect human life and ecological systems from harmful chemicals that could harm human health and pollute the environment. Furthermore, it may cause environmental concerns such as pollution, acid rain, deforestation and desertification, ozone layer depletion, and climate change. The use of nano biosensors for environmental surveillance may also result in the release of some organic compounds (carbon-based) that contain or comprise synthetic substances such as insecticides and polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), which can pollute the natural ecosystem. Considering this fact, it is relevant to say that while employing or exploiting NBS for monitoring the environment, it is necessary to establish an effective technique of handling, addressing, and discarding such wastes or organic pollutants contained in the nanomaterials or compounds that comprise NBS for environmental monitoring [249]. The failing of proper utilization of NBS for monitoring the environment not only poses a greater risk to ecological systems but also to humans, it may be subjected to the legal consequences of restricting the use of Nano biosensors. However, several of the important environmental legislative frameworks provided a problem or affected the use of NBS for environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration with emerging technologies (e.g., Internet of Things, machine learning)
ce:section.ce:para: The healthcare portable biosensor devices that use IoT still have a lot of potential for improvement, such as privacy of personal data, storage of data, sources of power, sparse uses for physiological disorders, distortion and noise minimization. Noise can be caused by a variety of factors, including frequent body movements, friction from hair and poor inconstancy between sensors and skin. Furthermore, artificial intelligence and assisted regression approaches can be used to track the progression of a range of prognostic markers. Secure communication at every stage is critical to protecting the user's data. Another challenge to wearable nano-based biosensors is a steady supply of electricity. Wearable gadgets have limited space, thus efficient energy sources, and effective battery management are necessary. Self-sustaining sensors including TENG (Triboelectric Nanogenerator) and PENG a (Piezoelectric Nanogenerator) are utilized as tiny energy-collecting types of equipment. The uses of biosensors that are worn for the diagnostic purpose of psychological ailments are quite limited in contrast to glucose, blood, and cardiovascular diseases. It will create space for researchers to investigate more about wearable sensors for detecting different illnesses. Considering the advancement of manufacturing techniques and gadget designs, there has been a surge in the market interest in multifunctional wearing nano-based biosensors. The rising market need necessitates sensors that are biocompatible, biodegradable, bioabsorbable, self-healing, and prudent to integrate with human and tissue interfaces. The functionality of biosensors is important for the accuracy and reliability of the produced data. From a medical perspective, nano biosensors that are biocompatible and recyclable work much more closely and safely with the skin interface. On the other hand, water-resistant and regeneration smartwatches can lessen their vulnerability to moisture, enhancing their dependability, security, and longevity [250].

The use of machine learning (ML) in portative and wearable biosensors provides value by handling an enormous quantity of biological sensing information and translating it into intelligible facts. For example, machine learning-enabled wearable biosensors can forecast an athlete's risk of injury, cardiac arrhythmia, and hydration status. As the need for precise and multipurpose sensors grows, machine learning-enabled devices appear to meet a wide range of healthcare market requirements. For example, using machine learning for biosensors allows for the development of smartphone applications for the immediate reading of sensor signals, allowing the device to be used for assessments conducted at home. Along with the benefits, machine learning-enabled devices confront several hurdles such as privacy of data, stability, consistency, accuracy, scalable manufacturing, and adaptive learning ability. As a result, scientific groups must overcome the aforementioned hurdles to move innovation from testing environments to clinical settings. A single biomolecule detection device cannot fully understand all diseases. An array of numerous biosensors and a display device combined with machine learning may be necessary to identify several analytes, generate more detailed knowledge, and enhance results. In this situation, advanced machine learning methods may identify multidimensional signal properties while minimizing noise, improving precision and responsiveness. Following, the combination of machine learning with nano biosensors has created worries regarding the disclosure of personal data. Thus, privacy of data concerns should be addressed collaboratively by service vendors, data managers, and consumers through the implementation of safer networks for exchanging data and proposed action. Creating a protected and trustworthy patient-preferred ML-enabled system can benefit both healthcare providers and patients. Professionals for healthcare and patients should collaborate to maximize the gains of ML-enabled solutions. ML efficacy in evaluating, extracting, and rendering vast amounts of data has the potential to change the healthcare business by eliminating errors, reducing workflow inadequacies, and improving illness diagnosis and monitoring. As a result, integrating machine learning with portative and health-monitoring biosensors is critical for developing advanced medical skills [251]. The AI in conjunction with the development of biosensors is used to promote commercialization and broaden its other applications. To accomplish education, self-organization, and flexibility in artificial intelligence-enabled biosensors, scientists must employed to develop flexible electronic devices that include the Internet of Things, artificial intelligence and chip technology. Flexible bioelectronic devices have exceptionally resilient mechanical qualities. It has advantages that mimic the human organs and body and low harm to tissues. Information is collected using sensor data provided by the algorithms of machine learning which are capable of monitoring essential body functions, detecting irregularities, and tracking medical therapy. Furthermore, cross-disciplinary applications of artificial intelligence can enhance sensing ability along with lowering prices. Shortly, artificial intelligence for medical biosensors will provide more creative solutions [252].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential for scaling up and commercialization
ce:section.ce:para: After multiple advancements, the nano biosensor has instilled a great deal of trust in the balance of technology and health care. In biomedicines, it is beneficial for the prompt identification of various diseases, allowing medical professionals to make well-informed choices depending on the preliminary test findings. Furthermore, nano biosensors are hitherto used as long-term trading goods and customized for specific applications. Nano-based biosensors can also improve a wide range of detections, including those in the pharmaceutical and industries related to food. In the food industry, substrates are mixed using biological reactors, which supply nutrients for a variety of uses that might be directed using nano-bio sensors. On a commercial level, nano biosensors can improve the separation and processing of a variety of commercial chemicals [253]. The field of nanotechnology has advanced rapidly, with several fascinating nanomaterials being widely used for diagnostic purposes with processed or adapted nano biosensors on various material substrates. Very few nanomaterials include graphene, quantum dots, nanocomposites, and carbon nanotubes. The first important use of the nano biosensor was to detect the presence of glucose. However, the marketing of nanobiosensors as a result has experienced significant challenges following the area's post-hype period. The most important difficulties to address before these technologies become commercially viable are emerging public and regulatory issues regarding the lack of international criteria for examining the efficacy of nano biosensors about healthcare ethical requirements [254]. Despite countless research efforts and developments, the hurdle to commercializing these sensors is the divide between academia as well as industry. The journey from innovation in science originating in foundational research into a commercial product, technique, or lengthy service is riddled with difficulties. However, cooperation among industries and academic studies is anticipated to accelerate the process of commercialization and produce economic and long-lasting nano biosensors. Point-of-care technology (POCT) has seen a significant increase in demand in recent years due to its quick, dependable, robust, and cost-effective capabilities for biomedical sample detection, testing, monitoring, and diagnosis. This request exists in the fields of foodborne disease and detection of bacteria, environmental pollution monitoring, and testing of biochemical. The commercial market has seen a significant surge in the creation of intelligent nano biosensors as a result of technological advancements incorporating Cyber-physical technologies and artificial intelligence. The nano biosensor's future application should be large, with a focus on integration, automation, and shrinking. Using integration and implementing modern technological elements such as the Internet of things, cloud computing, deep learning, cyber-physical systems, data analysis, and artificial intelligence (AI), the product can be commercialized. It primarily consists of three innovations: nano, biological, and sensor, which when combined generate a nano biosensor for POCT applications [255]. Fig. 13 sums up various challenges concerned with nanomaterials-based biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of nanomaterial-enabled biosensors in environmental monitoring and real-world applications and impact on sustainable development goals
ce:section.ce:para: Biosensors are current engineering gadgets that could be applied to a broad range of scientific applications. Biosensors are frequently used in various applications, such as manufacturing process control, military, monitoring the environment, medicine, microbiology, and food safety control. Biosensors can detect contamination from the environment, poisonous substances, bio-hazardous bacteria and viruses in organic matter, and biomolecules in medical diagnostics [256]. Furthermore, deep healthcare uses include well-being monitoring, long-term health treatment, and in vitro diagnostic investigations like infectious illness detection for early identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of nanomaterial-enabled biosensors in environmental monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The most challenging obstacles for the forthcoming years include pollution from plastics, eutrophication, forest destruction, pollution in the air, changing the climate, and treatment and eradication of fatberg. Outlined is an inventory of the most important nanomaterial-enabled biosensors to monitor plastic pollution. The majority of multispectral sensors were incapable of discerning between plastic kinds. The hyperspectral sensors produced exceptional findings by combining narrow shortwave infrared channels along with specialized absorbance bands, demonstrating their outstanding capacities for the inequality of plastic [257]. Microplastics (0.1 μm–1 mm) can be detected using an electrochemical-impedance-spectroscopy-based nanosensor that is generated by accumulating cyanobacterial extracellular polymeric substances (EPSs) derived from cyanobacteria on an electrode of gold [258]. Surface acoustic wave (SAW) detain lines at lithium niobate have improved device performance by optimizing specifications for detecting polystyrene particles as nanoplastics [259]. The split-ring resonator and coplanar waveguide microwave sensors demonstrated high signal-to-noise ratios and could track Real-time variations in comparative cell size. microwave sensing's promise as an adjunct method for microscale pollution screening and single-cell biophysical studies [260]. Flexible screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPEs) are used for detecting hydroquinone, bisphenol-A (BPA), and catechol in water with an easy electrochemical technique in acidic conditions. Screen-printed carbon electrodes are used to detect emerging contaminants from plastics in water and monitor the purity of the water [261]. Eutrophication is a method by which a surplus of nutrients and minerals, such as aggregation of phosphorus and nitrogen, from cultivation and wastewater discharge, resulting in the overwhelming surge of algae and various other aquatic organisms in water bodies. Paired Emitter-Detector Diodes outperformed usual photodiode-LED arrays in their spectral responsiveness, power consumption, cost, detection limit, and compliance with turbidity sensing standards, allowing the deployment of affordable and effective environment nano sensors for analyzing eutrophication [262]. An autonomous ceramic soil microbial fuel cell (MFC) sensor detects oxygen dissolved within the water to identify the growth of algae and eutrophication. MFC-based biosensors are used for the Initial detection of eutrophic occurrences, dissolved oxygen, and nitrates and focus on the impact of temperature and pH on the performance of the sensor [263].

The everlasting variations in Earth's weather forecast have been noted during the past decade and are anticipated to continue. Solar cells are an important part of the fight against the effects of climate change since they provide a renewable energy source. Photovoltaic (PV) modules can measure temperature and mechanical stress directly and continuously through the use of silicon solar-cell unified with temperature and stress sensors [264]. A pH sensor based on optics is composed of a fluorescent dye, which is pH-sensitive, implanted in a fluoro resin membrane filter. It allows the innocuous assessment of acetic acid and which is located in photovoltaic modules through the testing of damp heat. It effectively traced pH variations For a maximum of 2000 h and showed a meticulous reaction to acetic acid that was not impacted by moisture or heat [265]. The incorporation of a light zinc oxide covering onto fiber-optic nanostructures to serve as a detector for temperature for in situ supercapacitor observing helps in attaining a correlation coefficient and high sensitivity within the temperature range of 30 °C–90 °C [266].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of nanomaterial-enabled biosensors in real-world applications
ce:section.ce:para: Biosensor technology has advanced significantly in several industries, including food processing, biomedicine, and healthcare. Advancements in nanotechnology are closely linked to the generation of new biosensors and the production of diverse nanomaterials. Biosensors are used in various biomedical applications, including wearable and implantable medical devices, for establishing treatment protocols and promoting patient recovery [267]. Healthcare electronics developed a wearable biosensor with having flexible capacitor with an array of biological features such as accident alerts, remote control, pressure measurement and motion assessment. The decomposable polymers have developed as a novel biomaterials class that is suitable for sensing approaches. Biosensors have been promoted for the primary screening for cancer and bone diseases. Graphene's increased biosensing properties, such as its ability to easily interact with rings of carbon found in proteins, have been combined with TiO2 for the early identification of cancer. This multilayer plasmonic sensor was capable of detecting cells of cancer from the cervix, skin, adrenal glands, breasts, blood and with maximal angular sensitivity [268]. A hexagonal tightly packed polystyrene nanosphere a monolayer coated with gold and a portative Raman spectrometer were utilized to detect breast cancer using tears of humans [269]. A nano biosensor linked through a microfluidic system was developed to diagnose breast cancer by detecting an extracellular vesicle microRNA biomarker [270]. The miR-15a biological marker for rheumatoid arthritis detection could be detected by an SPR-based biosensor. Comparably, the identification of osteoarthritis has been demonstrated using an electrochemical biosensor that relied on polyhydrogel film [271]. Biosensors are employed in tissue engineering processes to arrange correct tissue outgrowth by measuring crucial parameters that involve oxygen and nutrient intake and metabolite production. An enzyme biosensor constructed using platinum wire is biocompatible. It is useful for monitoring the integrity of tissue implantation put into the tissues of the brain [272].

Electrochemical sensors can be used in vitro approaches that involve tracking the redox compounds generated by implants after biological insertion., in the past few years, amperometric sensors utilized in both in vitro and in vivo approaches to evaluate the quality of 3D constructs by observing the liberate various chemicals like dopamine, ascorbic acid, glucose, oxygen, and nitric oxide [247]. A few researchers developed a novel opto-microfluidic sensing technology for early finding of the COVID-19 virus utilizing human plasma. An attempt was made to enhance the detection capacity of a sensor based on optics by integrating the QDs advantages with microfluidic technology. Conjugating antibodies with QDs allowed researchers to identify two strains of influenza virus (H5 and H9). The QD-conjugated antibodies displayed strong fluorescence [273]. Biosensors have enormous promise for real-time microbiological diagnosis. The development of a flexible electrochemical immunological biosensor used for the observation of tumor necrosis factor (TNF), will also aid in wound healing monitoring. The immobilization of TNF was accomplished utilizing a differential pulse voltammetry technique [274]. The existence of E. coli drinking water was determined by an electrochemical immunological biosensor with a screen-printed electrode on a gold substrate. The rapid detection of numerous epidemic and pandemic pathogens utilizing biosensing techniques has lately gained popularity [275]. Phthalocyanines (Pc) and Porphyrins (Pp) are a specific type of macrocyclic organic substances with distinct photophysical characteristics that are used as fluorescence probes in biosensors, fluorescence imaging and drug delivery. In this respect, a few researchers developed a carboxyl Pp-based fluorescence biological sensor for identifying aflatoxin B1 utilized in pharmaceuticals [276]. Biosensors are useful for real-time clinical assessment of various chemicals in biofluids, primarily glucose. CuO nanoleaves were made using a simple hydrothermal method and altered with an electrode made of glassy carbon. The non-enzymatic electrode-based biosensor demonstrated excellent stability, reliability, and high glucose responsiveness [277]. A gold adornment is hierarchical. Cu nanoflower plated on GO nanofibers exhibited an excellent electrocatalytic capacity for reducing glucose to gluconic acid [278]. Biosensors are analytical instruments that detect biomarkers like DNA and proteins. Researchers developed a label-free and sensitive optical biosensor using Si nanowires to determine CRP in serum from humans. A gold-DNA-conjugated electrochemical biosensor identified pork content in consumable goods. The sensor detected pork in meals by identifying Sus scrofa mtDNA (pork DNA) present in uncooked and cooked meat items [247]. Nanobiosensors have a variety of applications in the development of novel technologies for sustainable agriculture practices. Nanobiosensors might be based on the gross and enormous reaction of crops in the entire field, dependent on smart detecting, or precise reaction based on the nanobionics method. Nanobionics are mathematical representations of sensors that detect the flows of various and signaling molecules and phytohormones. In general, biological sensors have attributed to the evolution of "smart agriculture" and/or "precise farming" due to their ability to perceive, process, and identify changes [279]. Precision agriculture offers a complete description of the information collected from field or soil conditions in order to make accurate decisions to obtain maximum output with minimal chemical fertilizer and pesticide inputs. Furthermore, smart plant systems aid in the rapid analysis and effective management of expensive agrochemicals, climate abiotic stress, and phytopathogens, all of which contribute to significant crop loss. Regular plant monitoring is a time-consuming process that can be done precisely with smart nano biosensors. Recently, nano biosensors were combined with information technology to aid farmers in rural places. Getting connected the nano biosensor to a global positioning system could greatly assist farmers in faraway regions in managing fertilizers, insecticides, pesticides, groundwater level, physical and chemical stresses, and identifying pathogens to prevent the development of numerous illnesses in on-site agricultural products in the fields [280]. Table 4 sums up the examples of nanomaterial-enabled biosensors in environmental monitoring and their real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this review, the details about various types of nanomaterial enabled biosensors and their importance for monitoring the environment have been discussed. Quantum dots, metal nanoparticles, and carbon based nanomaterials have been incorporated into biosensing platforms that has immensely improved the sensing and analysis of pollutants in the environment. These nanomaterials are providing a unique characteristic such as large surface area to volume ratio, availability of functional group, and low dimensionality which interrupt the biosensors efficiency in terms of sensitivity, selective and portability. From the results of this study, we can conclude that the use of these nanomaterials enables the development of the biosensors that can effectively detect very low concentrations of different pollutants in the environment. For instance, the graphene based immunosensor show the detection limit at the level of 0. 01 ppb for lead ions in water samples, and recently, researchers have developed biosensors containing gold nanoparticles reporting a high sensitivity of detection in case of mercury ions to the extent of 0. 005 ppb. They are important conduits for monitoring and passing of environmental status information required for combating pollution and for the protection of human life. The analytical performance of the nanomaterial based biosensors is well illustrated by their fast and real-time response coupled with high accuracies. Due to the nanoscale dimensions of the sensing elements it is possible to have a quick signal response, which is crucial for immediate identification and analysis of environmental samples and variations. These sensors you find as very small in size and this makes them very flexible and can be transported easily to any area whether it is in a city or in a remote place.

Further, the ability to use these biosensors in multiple ways of detecting various analytes like heavy metals, pathogens and pesticides infuse versatility and vast applicability of these biosensors in environmental analysis. Due to this, the ability to fabricate both singleplex and multiplex detection system makes it possible to detect many pollutants at once, making the process more of an all in one. Despite of these developments, some issues are still there such as stability issue, reproducibility issues and biocompatibility issues are still there. Solving these problems, as well as the inclusion of the IoT and ML into nanomaterial-based biosensors, will further improve existing applications and expand the number of possible uses of biosensors. It is also noteworthy that there is potential for concentrating and commercialization that would contribute to the extension and availability of the identified possibilities The presented potential enables a broader realization and application of the carried-out activities and achieved results. Due to their ability to detect emerging pollutants and being highly selective and portable, they are very useful in the assessment of environmental sustainability. The further enhancement and improvement of these technologies will undoubtedly help in the implementation of environmental goals around the world and preserving the environment and the health of people.
